cards.front: Adjacency stuck in EXSTART/EXCHANGE — most common cause and fix?
cards.back: MTU mismatch on the OSPF interfaces. Verify with `show ip ospf interface <int>` (compare MTU values). Fix: align MTUs (`ip mtu 1500`) on both ends; or enable `ip ospf mtu-ignore` as a workaround.

cards.front: Adjacency stuck in INIT state — what does it mean?
cards.back: A router has received a Hello from a neighbor that does NOT list itself in the Hello's neighbor field. Usually a one-way communication problem (ACL blocking inbound, broken multicast). Check both directions.

cards.front: Adjacency stays in 2-WAY state — is this a problem?
cards.back: Not necessarily. On multi-access (broadcast Ethernet) networks, DR and BDR form FULL with everyone, but non-DR/BDR pairs (DROther-DROther) stay at 2-WAY. That's normal and expected.

cards.front: What's the OSPF metric calculation rule for end-to-end path cost?
cards.back: Sum of OUTBOUND interface costs along the path. Cost = ref-bw / interface-bw. Inbound interface cost is NOT counted. Lowest total cost wins. Equal-cost paths → ECMP.

cards.front: How does OSPF rank routes when there are multiple paths to the same destination?
cards.back: (1) Intra-area (O) — within same area. (2) Inter-area (O IA) — through another area via ABR. (3) External Type 1 (E1) — redistributed, internal cost added to external cost. (4) External Type 2 (E2, default external) — only external cost considered. Order: O > O IA > E1 > E2 — regardless of total cost.

cards.front: What's the difference between OSPF E1 and E2 external routes?
cards.back: E1 (External Type 1): metric = external cost + internal OSPF cost to the ASBR. E2 (default): metric = external cost only (internal cost ignored). E1 reflects the true full-path cost; E2 makes the cost equal regardless of where in the OSPF domain you are.

cards.front: What command displays the OSPF database (LSDB)?
cards.back: `show ip ospf database` — lists all LSAs by type (router, network, summary, external). Useful for understanding what an area knows. `show ip ospf database router <RID>` drills into a specific router's LSA.

cards.front: OSPF default network types per interface: Ethernet/FDDI, PPP/HDLC, Frame Relay/X.25?
cards.back: Broadcast: Ethernet, FDDI (default). Point-to-point: PPP, HDLC. Non-broadcast (NBMA): Frame Relay, X.25. Broadcast = DR/BDR election + dynamic neighbor discovery + 10/40s timers. Point-to-point = no DR/BDR, dynamic discovery, 10/40s. NBMA = no DR/BDR by default, NO dynamic discovery (must configure neighbors manually), 30/120s timers. Mismatch in network type forms adjacency but no routes.

cards.front: Common OSPF LSA types tested on CCNA: Type 1, Type 2, Type 5.
cards.back: Type 1 (Router LSA): every router advertises its links + cost; flooded within an area only. Type 2 (Network LSA): generated by the DR on a multi-access segment, lists routers attached. Type 3 (Summary LSA): generated by ABRs, advertises an inter-area route. Type 5 (AS-External LSA): generated by an ASBR, advertises a route external to OSPF; flooded across all areas (except stub).

cards.front: Serial-link basics: HDLC default, DCE vs DTE, clock rate.
cards.back: Cisco serial interfaces default to cHDLC (Cisco's proprietary HDLC) encapsulation. DCE (Data Communications Equipment) provides the clock — `clock rate <bps>` is configured on the DCE side. DTE (Data Terminal Equipment) accepts the clock. In a lab back-to-back cable, one router is DCE, the other DTE. Verify which with `show controllers <int>`. Use `encapsulation ppp` to switch to PPP.

cards.front: Can two routers with the same OSPF Router-ID become neighbors?
cards.back: No — duplicate router IDs are not allowed. The OSPF process logs a 'duplicate router-id' error and the adjacency cannot form. The router-id is also used as the LSA originator; duplicates would corrupt the LSDB.
